methodology: attestral scanned the documented launch configuration (the mcpServers block users paste) of widely-used public MCP servers, not their source code. Aggregate only: no repository is named here; per-repo results are under responsible-disclosure embargo until maintainers are notified.
denominator_note: pct_of_repos_with_config = repos_affected / repos that committed a scannable MCP config or prompt file (23).

findings.title: One server name resolves to different launch targets
findings.caveat: Fires when the same server name appears with different launch targets across multiple committed example configs in one repo - a real ambiguity, but usually an artifact of many independent examples, not one deployed fleet. Review before headlining.

findings.title: Remote MCP server with no authentication
Lethal trifecta: private data + an outbound channel in one fleet
Shell-capable MCP server
findings.caveat: Literal-true, but a large share are the Windows `cmd /c npx ...` launcher idiom, not a server that intentionally exposes a shell tool. Review the split.

findings.title: Tool server holds cloud credentials (agent->cloud reach)
Shell execution + outbound network reach in one fleet
Toxic flow: untrusted input can reach a sensitive action
Filesystem server rooted at a broad path
findings.caveat: FIXED in attestral 0.6.0. The matcher previously substring-matched the '/' token (any arg with a slash - docker refs like ghcr.io/..., the Windows '/c' flag, URLs - tripped it), which massively over-counted. It now fires only on a true broad root: an arg that EQUALS or sits directly under /, ~, /home, or /Users (v == i or i.startswith(v + '/')). Counts are now honest. NOTE the single remaining hit in this sweep is a scoped '~/.aws' docker volume mount (AWS creds dir), not a whole-home root - narrow in practice; validate before headlining.

findings.title: Tool calls auto-approved with no human checkpoint
Persistent agent memory / vector store configured

new_rules_silent.note: The nine newest agentic rules fired on zero of the 33 servers. These are MCP servers; they do not ship capability declarations, coding-agent settings, registry manifests, or A2A cards, so there is nothing to match. A real-world false-positive read: the new rules did not spuriously fire on real code.
